Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SPQ-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.1) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.1 Moles and Molar Mass
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers moles and molar mass for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.1). Students should understand that the mole is the SI unit for amount of substance, defined as exactly 6.022 x 10^23 representative particles (Avogadro's number). Molar mass (g/mol) equals the atomic or molecular mass in amu and serves as the conversion factor between grams and moles. Students must convert fluently between mass, moles, and number of particles. Dimensional analysis is the essential problem-solving approach. Connect to stoichiometry, empirical and molecular formulas, and solution concentration. AP exam note: mole conversions appear in nearly every unit and are foundational for all quantitative chemistry. Vocabulary: mole, molar mass, Avogadro's number, representative particle, dimensional analysis, formula mass, molecular mass.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SPQ-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.2) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.2 Mass Spectra of Elements
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers mass spectra of elements for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.2). Students should understand that a mass spectrometer separates isotopes by mass-to-charge ratio, producing a mass spectrum where peak positions indicate isotope masses and peak heights (or areas) indicate relative abundances. The weighted average of isotope masses gives the average atomic mass on the periodic table. Students must calculate average atomic mass from isotopic data and interpret mass spectra to determine isotopic composition. Connect to atomic structure, nuclear chemistry, and how elemental identity is defined by proton number while mass varies with neutron number. AP exam note: interpreting mass spectra and calculating average atomic mass are commonly tested skills. Vocabulary: isotope, mass spectrum, mass-to-charge ratio, relative abundance, weighted average, atomic mass unit.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SPQ-2.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.3) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.3 Elemental Composition of Pure Substances
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers elemental composition of pure substances for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.3). Students should understand how to determine empirical and molecular formulas from percent composition or combustion analysis data. The empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms; the molecular formula gives the actual number of atoms and is a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula. To find the empirical formula: convert percent to grams, then to moles, then divide by the smallest mole value. Students must also calculate percent composition from a chemical formula. Connect to molar mass determination, stoichiometry, and molecular structure. AP exam note: empirical and molecular formula calculations are classic free-response problems. Vocabulary: empirical formula, molecular formula, percent composition, combustion analysis, mole ratio.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SPQ-2.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.4) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.4 Composition of Mixtures
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers composition of mixtures for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-2.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.4). Students should understand that mixtures contain two or more substances that retain their individual properties and can be separated by physical means. The composition of a mixture can be determined using elemental analysis, where the mass or mole fraction of each component is calculated. Students must distinguish between pure substances and mixtures, and between homogeneous (solutions) and heterogeneous mixtures. Analytical techniques such as gravimetric and volumetric analysis can determine mixture composition quantitatively. Connect to solution chemistry, separation techniques, and stoichiometry of reactions involving impure samples. AP exam note: questions may involve calculating the percent purity of a sample or determining composition from reaction data. Vocabulary: mixture, pure substance, homogeneous, heterogeneous, mass percent, mole fraction, gravimetric analysis.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-1.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.5) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.5 Atomic Structure and Electron Configuration
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers atomic structure and electron configuration for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-1.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.5). Students should understand the quantum mechanical model of the atom: electrons occupy orbitals (s, p, d, f) organized into energy levels and sublevels. Electron configurations follow the Aufbau principle (fill lowest energy first), Hund's rule (maximize unpaired electrons in degenerate orbitals), and the Pauli exclusion principle (max 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins). Students must write full and condensed electron configurations, identify valence electrons, and relate configuration to an element's position on the periodic table. Ions form by losing or gaining electrons, and transition metals may have irregular configurations (Cr, Cu). Connect to periodic trends, chemical bonding, and reactivity. AP exam note: electron configuration and its relationship to properties are foundational. Vocabulary: orbital, electron configuration, quantum number, Aufbau principle, Hund's rule, Pauli exclusion, valence electron, core electron.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-1.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.6) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.6 Photoelectron Spectroscopy
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers photoelectron spectroscopy (PES) for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-1.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.6). Students should understand that PES uses high-energy photons to eject electrons from atoms, measuring their kinetic energies to determine binding energies. A PES spectrum shows peaks at different binding energies: peaks farther left (higher binding energy) represent core electrons closer to the nucleus, while peaks farther right represent valence electrons. Peak height (relative signal) indicates the number of electrons in that sublevel. Students must interpret PES spectra to confirm electron configurations and identify elements. The relationship is: photon energy = binding energy + kinetic energy. Connect to electron configuration, atomic structure, and periodic trends. AP exam note: PES spectrum interpretation is a distinctive AP Chemistry skill. Vocabulary: photoelectron spectroscopy, binding energy, kinetic energy, ionization energy, sublevel, core electrons, valence electrons.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-2.A (Unit 1, Topic 1.7) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.7 Periodic Trends
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers periodic trends for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-2.A, Unit 1, Topic 1.7). Students should understand the major periodic trends and their underlying causes based on effective nuclear charge (Zeff) and electron shielding. Ionization energy, electronegativity, and electron affinity generally increase across a period (increasing Zeff) and decrease down a group (increasing atomic radius and shielding). Atomic radius decreases across a period and increases down a group. Ionic radius follows specific patterns: cations are smaller than parent atoms, anions are larger. Students must explain trends using Coulomb's law and the concept of effective nuclear charge. Connect to chemical bonding, reactivity, and compound properties. AP exam note: explaining trends with reference to Zeff and shielding is essential. Vocabulary: ionization energy, electronegativity, atomic radius, ionic radius, effective nuclear charge, electron shielding, Coulomb's law.

Atomic Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-2.B (Unit 1, Topic 1.8) Atomic Structure and Properties - 1.8 Valence Electrons and Ionic Compounds
Atomic Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers valence electrons and ionic compounds for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-2.B, Unit 1, Topic 1.8). Students should understand that ionic compounds form when metals transfer valence electrons to nonmetals, creating cations and anions held together by electrostatic attraction (ionic bonds). The resulting crystal lattice maximizes attractive forces between oppositely charged ions while minimizing repulsion. Lattice energy depends on ion charge and size (Coulomb's law): higher charges and smaller ions produce stronger lattice energy. Students must predict ionic compound formulas from ion charges and relate physical properties (high melting points, brittleness, conductivity when dissolved or molten) to ionic bonding. Connect to periodic trends, electron configuration, and energy changes in bond formation. AP exam note: relating ionic compound properties to Coulombic interactions is key. Vocabulary: ionic bond, lattice energy, crystal lattice, cation, anion, electrostatic attraction, Coulomb's law, valence electron.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-3.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.1) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.1 Types of Chemical Bonds
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers types of chemical bonds for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-3.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.1). Students should understand the three main bond types: ionic (electron transfer between metal and nonmetal), covalent (electron sharing between nonmetals), and metallic (delocalized electron sea among metal atoms). Bond type is predicted by electronegativity difference: large differences favor ionic bonding, small differences favor covalent bonding. The properties of substances (melting point, conductivity, solubility, hardness) relate directly to bond type. Students must classify bonds, predict properties, and explain the continuum from purely covalent to purely ionic. Connect to molecular structure, intermolecular forces, and material properties. AP exam note: comparing and contrasting bond types and predicting substance properties is common. Vocabulary: ionic bond, covalent bond, metallic bond, electronegativity, electron transfer, electron sharing, delocalized electrons, network covalent.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-3.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.2) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.2 Intramolecular Force and Potential Energy
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers intramolecular force and potential energy for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-3.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.2). Students should understand that bond formation releases energy and bond breaking requires energy. The potential energy diagram of a bond shows minimum energy at the bond length (equilibrium distance). Bond energy (bond dissociation energy) is the energy required to break one mole of bonds in the gas phase. Stronger bonds have larger bond energies and shorter bond lengths. Single bonds are longest and weakest; triple bonds are shortest and strongest. Students must use potential energy diagrams to compare bond strength and relate bond order to bond energy and length. Connect to thermochemistry, enthalpy calculations, and reaction energetics. AP exam note: interpreting potential energy curves and comparing bond strengths are tested skills. Vocabulary: bond energy, bond length, bond order, potential energy, equilibrium distance, endothermic, exothermic.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-3.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.3) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.3 Structure of Ionic Solids
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers the structure of ionic solids for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-3.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.3). Students should understand that ionic solids consist of a three-dimensional crystal lattice of alternating cations and anions. The structure maximizes electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions. Properties include high melting and boiling points, brittleness (when stressed, like charges align and repel), hardness, and electrical conductivity only when dissolved in water or molten (ions must be free to move). Lattice energy increases with higher ion charges and smaller ionic radii (Coulomb's law). Students must predict relative melting points of ionic compounds from ion charges and sizes. Connect to solubility, dissolution energetics, and ionic bonding. AP exam note: ranking ionic compound properties using Coulomb's law is a key skill. Vocabulary: crystal lattice, lattice energy, Coulomb's law, electrostatic attraction, brittleness, conductivity, dissociation.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-3.D (Unit 2, Topic 2.4) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.4 Structure of Metals and Alloys
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers the structure of metals and alloys for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-3.D, Unit 2, Topic 2.4). Students should understand the electron sea model: metal atoms release valence electrons into a delocalized pool that holds positively charged metal cations together. This model explains metallic properties: electrical and thermal conductivity (free-moving electrons), malleability and ductility (layers of atoms slide past each other without breaking bonds), and luster (electrons absorb and re-emit light). Alloys are mixtures of metals (or metals with nonmetals) that have modified properties: substitutional alloys replace atoms of similar size, while interstitial alloys place smaller atoms in gaps between larger ones. Students must relate the electron sea model to observed metallic properties. Connect to bonding theory and material science. AP exam note: explaining metallic properties using the electron sea model is tested. Vocabulary: metallic bond, electron sea model, delocalized electrons, malleability, ductility, conductivity, alloy, substitutional, interstitial.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-4.A (Unit 2, Topic 2.5) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.5 Lewis Diagrams
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers Lewis diagrams for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-4.A, Unit 2, Topic 2.5). Students should understand that Lewis structures represent valence electrons as dots or lines (bonding pairs) around atomic symbols. To draw Lewis structures: count total valence electrons, place the least electronegative atom as central, distribute electrons to satisfy the octet rule (or duet for hydrogen). Some atoms form multiple bonds (double, triple) to satisfy octets. Exceptions to the octet rule include molecules with odd electrons (NO), incomplete octets (BF3), and expanded octets (PCl5, SF6) for Period 3+ elements using d orbitals. Students must draw accurate Lewis structures for molecules and polyatomic ions. Connect to molecular geometry, polarity, and intermolecular forces. AP exam note: Lewis structure drawing is foundational for VSEPR and polarity analysis. Vocabulary: Lewis structure, valence electrons, lone pair, bonding pair, octet rule, multiple bond, expanded octet, formal charge.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-4.B (Unit 2, Topic 2.6) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.6 Resonance and Formal Charge
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers resonance and formal charge for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-4.B, Unit 2, Topic 2.6). Students should understand that resonance occurs when multiple valid Lewis structures can be drawn for a molecule by moving electron pairs (not atoms). The true structure is a resonance hybrid, an average of all contributing structures. Resonance stabilizes molecules by delocalizing electrons. Formal charge (FC = valence electrons - lone pair electrons - bonding electrons/2) helps identify the most plausible Lewis structure: the best structure minimizes formal charges and places negative formal charges on more electronegative atoms. Students must draw resonance structures, calculate formal charges, and identify the most stable contributor. Connect to molecular stability, bond order, and reactivity. AP exam note: resonance and formal charge analysis are frequently tested together. Vocabulary: resonance, resonance hybrid, formal charge, delocalized electrons, contributing structure, bond order.

Compound Structure and Properties.skill: SAP-4.C (Unit 2, Topic 2.7) Compound Structure and Properties - 2.7 VSEPR and Hybridization
Compound Structure and Properties.prompt: This topic covers VSEPR theory and hybridization for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-4.C, Unit 2, Topic 2.7). Students should understand that VSEPR (Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion) theory predicts molecular geometry by minimizing repulsion between electron domains around the central atom. Key geometries include linear (2 domains), trigonal planar (3), tetrahedral (4), trigonal bipyramidal (5), and octahedral (6). Lone pairs occupy more space than bonding pairs, altering bond angles and molecular shape (e.g., bent, trigonal pyramidal). Hybridization (sp, sp2, sp3, sp3d, sp3d2) corresponds to the number of electron domains. Molecular polarity depends on both bond polarity and molecular geometry: symmetric molecules may be nonpolar despite polar bonds. Students must predict geometry, hybridization, and polarity from Lewis structures. AP exam note: VSEPR predictions are heavily tested. Vocabulary: VSEPR, electron domain, molecular geometry, hybridization, bond angle, polar, nonpolar, dipole moment.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-5.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.1) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.1 Intermolecular and Interparticle Forces
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers intermolecular forces for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-5.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules, much weaker than intramolecular (covalent/ionic) bonds. Types ranked by strength: London dispersion forces (LDF, present in all molecules, increase with molar mass and surface area), dipole-dipole forces (between polar molecules), and hydrogen bonding (strong dipole-dipole between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on N, O, or F). Ion-dipole forces occur between ions and polar molecules in solution. Students must identify the strongest IMF present in a substance and use IMFs to explain physical properties (boiling point, vapor pressure, viscosity, surface tension). Connect to phase changes, solubility, and solution formation. AP exam note: predicting and comparing physical properties using IMFs is a core skill. Vocabulary: London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding, ion-dipole, intermolecular force, vapor pressure, boiling point.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-5.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.2) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.2 Properties of Solids
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers properties of solids for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-5.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand the four types of crystalline solids and their properties. Ionic solids: high melting points, brittle, conduct electricity when dissolved. Molecular solids: low melting points, soft, poor conductors, held by IMFs. Covalent network solids (diamond, SiO2, SiC): very high melting points, very hard, poor conductors (except graphite). Metallic solids: variable melting points, malleable, good conductors. Amorphous solids lack long-range order. Students must classify solids by type and predict properties based on the dominant interparticle forces. Connect to bonding, IMFs, and material properties. AP exam note: classifying and comparing solid types based on properties is commonly tested. Vocabulary: ionic solid, molecular solid, covalent network solid, metallic solid, amorphous, crystalline, lattice, melting point.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-6.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.3) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.3 Solids, Liquids, and Gases
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers solids, liquids, and gases for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-6.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand how particle behavior differs across phases: solids have fixed shape and volume with particles vibrating in place; liquids have fixed volume but variable shape with particles sliding past each other; gases have variable shape and volume with particles in rapid random motion. Phase changes (melting, boiling, sublimation, deposition, condensation, freezing) involve energy changes without temperature change at transition points. Heating curves show temperature vs. energy added, with plateaus at phase transitions. Students must interpret heating curves and relate phase behavior to intermolecular forces. Connect to IMFs, thermochemistry, and kinetic molecular theory. AP exam note: heating curve analysis and relating phase properties to IMF strength are tested. Vocabulary: phase, phase change, heating curve, enthalpy of fusion, enthalpy of vaporization, sublimation, vapor pressure.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-7.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.4) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.4 Ideal Gas Law
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers the ideal gas law for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that PV = nRT relates pressure (P, atm), volume (V, L), moles (n), the ideal gas constant (R = 0.0821 L-atm/mol-K), and temperature (T, Kelvin). The ideal gas law assumes particles have negligible volume and no intermolecular forces. Students must solve for any variable, convert units appropriately (especially temperature to Kelvin), and apply the combined gas law for changing conditions. At STP (0 degrees C, 1 atm), one mole of ideal gas occupies 22.4 L. Dalton's law of partial pressures states that total pressure equals the sum of partial pressures. Students must calculate molar mass and density of gases. Connect to stoichiometry, kinetic molecular theory, and real gas behavior. AP exam note: ideal gas law calculations appear frequently. Vocabulary: ideal gas law, STP, partial pressure, Dalton's law, molar volume, gas constant.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-7.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.5) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.5 Kinetic Molecular Theory
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers kinetic molecular theory (KMT) for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand the postulates of KMT: gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are perfectly elastic, particle volume is negligible compared to container volume, there are no intermolecular forces, and average kinetic energy is proportional to absolute temperature (KE_avg = 3/2 kT). At the same temperature, lighter gases have higher average speeds (KE = 1/2 mv^2). Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curves show the range of molecular speeds at different temperatures. Students must use KMT to explain gas behavior (pressure, temperature, effusion) and interpret distribution curves. Connect to ideal gas law, diffusion, effusion, and Graham's law. AP exam note: interpreting Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions is a key skill. Vocabulary: kinetic molecular theory, elastic collision, average kinetic energy, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, root mean square speed, effusion, diffusion.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-7.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.6) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.6 Deviation from Ideal Gas Law
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers deviations from ideal gas behavior for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that real gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressures (particles closer together, volume becomes significant) and low temperatures (particles move slowly, IMFs become significant). The van der Waals equation (P + a(n/V)^2)(V - nb) = nRT corrects for IMFs (a) and particle volume (b). Gases with stronger IMFs and larger particle sizes deviate more. At high temperatures and low pressures, real gases behave most ideally. Students must predict when and why deviations occur and identify which gases deviate most. Connect to IMFs, molecular size, and conditions of gas behavior. AP exam note: explaining conditions for ideal vs. real gas behavior is commonly tested. Vocabulary: real gas, ideal gas, van der Waals equation, compressibility factor, intermolecular forces, particle volume, deviation.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SPQ-3.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.7) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.7 Solutions and Mixtures
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers solutions and mixtures for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that solutions are homogeneous mixtures with a solute dissolved in a solvent. Concentration can be expressed as molarity (M = mol solute / L solution), mass percent, mole fraction, or molality. Dilution follows M1V1 = M2V2. The dissolution process involves solute-solute, solvent-solvent, and solute-solvent interactions: dissolution is favored when solute-solvent interactions are comparable to or stronger than the original interactions (like dissolves like). Students must perform concentration calculations, dilution problems, and predict solubility based on polarity. Connect to colligative properties, reaction stoichiometry in solution, and intermolecular forces. AP exam note: molarity calculations and dilution problems are foundational. Vocabulary: solution, solute, solvent, molarity, dilution, concentration, homogeneous mixture, dissolution.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SPQ-3.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.8) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.8 Representations of Solutions
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers representations of solutions for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand how to represent solutions at the particulate level using diagrams that show solute particles (molecules or ions) distributed among solvent molecules. For ionic compounds, dissolution produces separated hydrated ions (not ion pairs or formula units). For molecular solutes, intact molecules are dispersed. Strong electrolytes fully dissociate (strong acids, strong bases, soluble salts); weak electrolytes partially dissociate; nonelectrolytes do not dissociate. Students must draw and interpret particulate diagrams of solutions and determine the species present. Connect to electrolyte behavior, conductivity, colligative properties, and net ionic equations. AP exam note: particulate diagram interpretation is a distinctive AP Chemistry skill. Vocabulary: electrolyte, nonelectrolyte, dissociation, hydration, ion-dipole interaction, particulate diagram, strong electrolyte, weak electrolyte.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SPQ-3.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.9) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.9 Separation of Solutions and Mixtures
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers separation of solutions and mixtures for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that mixtures can be separated based on differences in physical properties of their components. Techniques include distillation (separates by boiling point differences), filtration (separates solids from liquids), chromatography (separates by differential affinity for stationary and mobile phases), and evaporation or crystallization (removes solvent to recover solute). Choosing the appropriate technique depends on the components' properties: volatility, solubility, particle size, and polarity. Students must select and justify separation techniques for given mixtures. Connect to solution properties, intermolecular forces, and laboratory skills. AP exam note: questions may describe a mixture and ask which separation method is appropriate. Vocabulary: distillation, filtration, chromatography, evaporation, crystallization, stationary phase, mobile phase, volatile.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SPQ-3.D (Unit 3, Topic 3.10) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.10 Solubility
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers solubility for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.10). Students should understand that solubility depends on intermolecular interactions between solute and solvent (like dissolves like): polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents, nonpolar in nonpolar. Temperature generally increases solubility of solids in liquids but decreases solubility of gases in liquids (Henry's law). Pressure affects gas solubility (Henry's law: S = kH * P). Saturated solutions are at equilibrium with undissolved solute; unsaturated can dissolve more; supersaturated contain more than equilibrium amount. Students must predict relative solubilities and explain using IMFs and thermodynamic considerations. Connect to solution equilibria, precipitation reactions, and Ksp. AP exam note: predicting and explaining solubility trends is tested. Vocabulary: solubility, saturated, unsaturated, supersaturated, Henry's law, miscible, immiscible, like dissolves like.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-8.A (Unit 3, Topic 3.11) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.11 Spectroscopy and the Electromagnetic Spectrum
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers spectroscopy and the electromagnetic spectrum for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.11). Students should understand that electromagnetic radiation has both wave and particle properties. The spectrum ranges from radio waves (low energy, long wavelength) to gamma rays (high energy, short wavelength). Key relationships: c = wavelength times frequency, and E = h times frequency (Planck's equation). Different regions of the EM spectrum interact with matter differently: UV-Vis causes electronic transitions, IR causes molecular vibrations, and microwave causes molecular rotations. Spectroscopy identifies substances by their characteristic absorption or emission patterns. Students must relate wavelength, frequency, and energy, and explain how spectroscopy provides structural information. Connect to atomic emission, Beer-Lambert law, and quantum mechanics. AP exam note: energy-frequency-wavelength calculations are foundational. Vocabulary: electromagnetic spectrum, wavelength, frequency, photon, Planck's constant, absorption, emission, spectroscopy.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-8.B (Unit 3, Topic 3.12) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.12 Properties of Photons
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers properties of photons for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.12). Students should understand that light behaves as both a wave and a particle (wave-particle duality). Photons are discrete packets of electromagnetic energy with energy E = hf = hc/wavelength, where h is Planck's constant. When atoms absorb photons, electrons transition to higher energy levels; when they emit photons, electrons drop to lower levels. The energy of the photon exactly equals the difference between energy levels. This explains atomic emission spectra (bright lines) and absorption spectra (dark lines). Students must calculate photon energy and relate it to electronic transitions. Connect to electron configuration, atomic spectra, and the Bohr model. AP exam note: photon energy calculations and connecting them to electronic transitions are key skills. Vocabulary: photon, wave-particle duality, quantized, energy level, electronic transition, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Planck's constant.

Properties of Substances and Mixtures.skill: SAP-8.C (Unit 3, Topic 3.13) Properties of Substances and Mixtures - 3.13 Beer-Lambert Law
Properties of Substances and Mixtures.prompt: This topic covers the Beer-Lambert law for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.13). Students should understand that Beer's law relates the absorbance (A) of a solution to its concentration: A = epsilon times b times c, where epsilon is the molar absorptivity, b is the path length, and c is the concentration. Absorbance is proportional to concentration for dilute solutions. A calibration curve (absorbance vs. concentration plot) allows determination of unknown concentrations by interpolation. Transmittance (T) relates to absorbance: A = -log(T). Colorimetry and spectrophotometry use this principle for quantitative analysis. Students must construct and interpret calibration curves and calculate unknown concentrations. Connect to solution properties, spectroscopy, and laboratory techniques. AP exam note: Beer's law calculations and calibration curve interpretation are commonly tested. Vocabulary: absorbance, transmittance, molar absorptivity, path length, calibration curve, Beer-Lambert law, spectrophotometer.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-1.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.1) Chemical Reactions - 4.1 Introduction for Reactions
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic introduces chemical reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-1.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.1). Students should understand that chemical reactions involve the rearrangement of atoms through breaking and forming of bonds. A balanced chemical equation represents conservation of mass: atoms of each element are equal on both sides. Students must balance equations by adjusting coefficients (not subscripts) and classify reactions as synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, or combustion. Physical evidence of reactions includes color change, gas production, precipitate formation, temperature change, and odor. Students must distinguish chemical changes from physical changes. Connect to stoichiometry, reaction types, and conservation laws. AP exam note: balanced equations are the starting point for nearly all quantitative problems. Vocabulary: reactant, product, coefficient, balanced equation, conservation of mass, synthesis, decomposition, combustion.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-1.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.2) Chemical Reactions - 4.2 Net Ionic Equations
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers net ionic equations for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-1.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.2). Students should understand the three forms of aqueous reaction equations: molecular (complete formula), complete ionic (all strong electrolytes shown as ions), and net ionic (only species that undergo change, with spectator ions removed). Solubility rules predict which ionic compounds are soluble or insoluble. Strong acids, strong bases, and soluble salts dissociate completely in water. Students must write molecular, complete ionic, and net ionic equations for precipitation, acid-base, and oxidation-reduction reactions. Spectator ions appear unchanged on both sides and are removed. Connect to solubility, electrolyte behavior, and reaction types. AP exam note: writing net ionic equations is a core skill tested in free-response questions. Vocabulary: molecular equation, complete ionic equation, net ionic equation, spectator ion, precipitate, solubility rules, strong electrolyte.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-1.C (Unit 4, Topic 4.3) Chemical Reactions - 4.3 Representations of Reactions
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers representations of reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-1.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.3). Students should understand that chemical reactions can be represented at three levels: macroscopic (observable changes), particulate (molecular-level diagrams showing atoms and molecules), and symbolic (chemical equations). Particulate diagrams must show conservation of atoms, correct molecular formulas, and appropriate phase representations. Students must translate between these three representations and identify discrepancies. Balanced equations convey mole ratios, physical states (s, l, g, aq), and energy changes. Students must interpret and draw particulate diagrams for reactions and connect them to balanced equations. Connect to stoichiometry, reaction types, and laboratory observations. AP exam note: particulate diagram interpretation is heavily emphasized. Vocabulary: macroscopic, particulate, symbolic, balanced equation, conservation of atoms, phase notation, mole ratio.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-1.D (Unit 4, Topic 4.4) Chemical Reactions - 4.4 Physical and Chemical Changes
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers physical and chemical changes for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-1.D, Unit 4, Topic 4.4). Students should understand that physical changes alter the form or state of matter without changing its chemical identity (phase changes, dissolving, mixing). Chemical changes produce new substances with different properties through bond breaking and formation. Evidence of chemical change includes energy changes, gas production, precipitate formation, color changes, and irreversibility. At the particulate level, physical changes rearrange particles without altering their composition, while chemical changes rearrange atoms into new combinations. Students must distinguish between physical and chemical changes and support their reasoning with evidence. Connect to conservation of mass, phase diagrams, and thermochemistry. AP exam note: distinguishing physical from chemical changes at the particulate level is tested. Vocabulary: physical change, chemical change, phase change, conservation of mass, dissolving, bond breaking, bond formation.

This topic covers stoichiometry for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-4.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.5). Students should understand that stoichiometry uses mole ratios from balanced chemical equations to relate amounts of reactants and products. Key concepts: converting between mass, moles, and particles using molar mass and Avogadro's number; using coefficients as mole ratios; identifying limiting and excess reagents; calculating theoretical yield, actual yield, and percent yield. The problem-solving sequence: balanced equation, then mole ratio, then moles of desired substance, then convert to requested units. Students must be able to balance equations and recognize that conservation of mass underlies all stoichiometric calculations. Connect to gas stoichiometry (using PV=nRT) and solution stoichiometry (using molarity M=mol/L). AP exam note: stoichiometry appears in both multiple-choice calculations and free-response experimental design questions. Vocabulary: stoichiometry, mole ratio, limiting reagent, excess reagent, theoretical yield, percent yield, molar mass, Avogadro's number.

Chemical Reactions.skill: SPQ-4.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.6) Chemical Reactions - 4.6 Introduction to Titration
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers titration for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-4.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.6). Students should understand that titration is a technique for determining the concentration of an unknown solution by reacting it with a solution of known concentration (standard solution). At the equivalence point, moles of acid equal moles of base (for a 1:1 reaction) according to stoichiometry. Indicators change color near the equivalence point, and the endpoint is when the indicator changes. Students must perform titration calculations using M1V1 relationships adjusted for stoichiometric ratios, and distinguish between equivalence point and endpoint. Connect to acid-base reactions, stoichiometry, and laboratory techniques. AP exam note: titration calculations and interpretation of titration curves appear in later units. Vocabulary: titration, titrant, analyte, equivalence point, endpoint, indicator, standard solution, buret.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-2.A (Unit 4, Topic 4.7) Chemical Reactions - 4.7 Types of Chemical Reactions
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers types of chemical reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-2.A, Unit 4, Topic 4.7). Students should understand the main categories: synthesis (A + B -> AB), decomposition (AB -> A + B), single replacement (A + BC -> AC + B, governed by activity series), double replacement (AB + CD -> AD + CB, including precipitation and neutralization), and combustion (hydrocarbon + O2 -> CO2 + H2O). The activity series predicts whether single replacement reactions occur. Driving forces for double replacement include precipitate formation, water formation, or gas production. Students must predict products and determine if a reaction will occur. Connect to solubility rules, acid-base chemistry, and redox reactions. AP exam note: predicting products and reaction types is a key analytical skill. Vocabulary: synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, activity series, driving force, precipitate.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-2.B (Unit 4, Topic 4.8) Chemical Reactions - 4.8 Introduction to Acid-Base Reactions
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic introduces acid-base reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-2.B, Unit 4, Topic 4.8). Students should understand that Arrhenius acids produce H+ in water and bases produce OH-. Bronsted-Lowry theory expands this: acids donate protons and bases accept protons. In a neutralization reaction, acid + base produce salt + water. Strong acids (HCl, HNO3, H2SO4, HBr, HI, HClO4) and strong bases (Group 1 and 2 hydroxides) completely dissociate. Conjugate acid-base pairs differ by one proton. Students must identify acids, bases, and conjugate pairs, write neutralization equations, and predict products. Connect to pH, buffer chemistry, and titration. AP exam note: identifying Bronsted-Lowry pairs and writing acid-base reactions are foundational for Units 8 and beyond. Vocabulary: Arrhenius acid/base, Bronsted-Lowry acid/base, conjugate pair, neutralization, proton donor, proton acceptor, strong acid, strong base.

Chemical Reactions.skill: TRA-2.C (Unit 4, Topic 4.9) Chemical Reactions - 4.9 Oxidation-Reduction (Redox) Reactions
Chemical Reactions.prompt: This topic covers oxidation-reduction reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-2.C, Unit 4, Topic 4.9). Students should understand that redox reactions involve the transfer of electrons between species. Oxidation is loss of electrons (increase in oxidation number); reduction is gain of electrons (decrease in oxidation number). OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain. The oxidizing agent is reduced; the reducing agent is oxidized. Students must assign oxidation numbers using rules (elements = 0, monatomic ions = charge, O = -2, H = +1 in most compounds), identify what is oxidized and reduced, and balance simple redox equations. Connect to electrochemistry, activity series, and energy production. AP exam note: identifying oxidation and reduction and assigning oxidation states are tested skills. Vocabulary: oxidation, reduction, oxidizing agent, reducing agent, oxidation number, electron transfer, half-reaction, redox.

Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers reaction rates for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-3.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.1). Students should understand that reaction rate measures how quickly reactant concentrations decrease or product concentrations increase over time. Rate = change in concentration / change in time, and is always expressed as a positive value. Rate depends on the nature of reactants, concentration, temperature, surface area, and presence of catalysts. Rates can be measured by monitoring color changes, pressure changes, mass changes, or volume of gas produced. Average rate is calculated over an interval; instantaneous rate is the slope of the tangent to the concentration-time curve at a specific point. Students must calculate and compare reaction rates from data. Connect to rate laws, collision theory, and reaction mechanisms. AP exam note: interpreting concentration-time data is foundational. Vocabulary: reaction rate, average rate, instantaneous rate, rate of disappearance, rate of appearance, concentration-time graph.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-3.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.2) Kinetics - 5.2 Introduction to Rate Law
Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers rate law for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-3.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.2). Students should understand that the rate law expresses rate as a function of reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]^m[B]^n, where k is the rate constant and m, n are reaction orders determined experimentally (not from coefficients). The overall order is m + n. To determine orders, use the method of initial rates: compare experiments where one concentration changes while others are held constant. Doubling concentration and observing rate change reveals order (rate doubles = first order, quadruples = second order, unchanged = zero order). Students must determine rate laws from experimental data and calculate rate constants with correct units. Connect to integrated rate laws, reaction mechanisms, and temperature dependence. AP exam note: determining rate law from initial rate data is heavily tested. Vocabulary: rate law, rate constant, reaction order, overall order, method of initial rates, first order, second order, zero order.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-3.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.3) Kinetics - 5.3 Concentration Changes Over Time
Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers concentration changes over time for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-3.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.3). Students should understand integrated rate laws that relate concentration to time. For zero order: [A] = [A]0 - kt (linear plot of [A] vs. t). For first order: ln[A] = ln[A]0 - kt (linear plot of ln[A] vs. t), with half-life t1/2 = 0.693/k. For second order: 1/[A] = 1/[A]0 + kt (linear plot of 1/[A] vs. t). The order is determined by which plot yields a straight line. Half-life is constant only for first-order reactions. Students must determine reaction order from graphical data, calculate half-life, and use integrated rate laws to find concentrations at given times. Connect to rate laws, radioactive decay (first order), and pharmacokinetics. AP exam note: graphical analysis of kinetic data is commonly tested. Vocabulary: integrated rate law, half-life, zero order, first order, second order, linear plot, rate constant.

This topic covers elementary reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-4.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.4). Students should understand that elementary reactions are single-step processes whose rate laws can be written directly from their molecularity. Unimolecular reactions (A -> products) are first order; bimolecular reactions (A + B -> products or 2A -> products) are second order; termolecular reactions are rare. For elementary steps only, the exponents in the rate law equal the stoichiometric coefficients. Complex reactions consist of multiple elementary steps forming a reaction mechanism. Students must distinguish elementary reactions from overall reactions and write rate laws for elementary steps. Connect to reaction mechanisms, rate-determining step, and catalysis. AP exam note: writing rate laws for elementary steps vs. overall reactions is a critical distinction. Vocabulary: elementary reaction, molecularity, unimolecular, bimolecular, termolecular, rate law, stoichiometric coefficient.

This topic covers the collision model for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-4.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.5). Students should understand that for a reaction to occur, particles must collide with sufficient energy (at least the activation energy, Ea) and proper orientation. Not all collisions are effective. Increasing temperature increases both collision frequency and the fraction of molecules with energy exceeding Ea, dramatically increasing reaction rate. The Arrhenius equation k = Ae^(-Ea/RT) quantifies the temperature dependence of the rate constant, where A is the frequency factor. Students must explain how concentration, temperature, surface area, and catalysts affect reaction rate using collision theory. Connect to kinetic molecular theory, activation energy, and catalysis. AP exam note: applying collision theory to explain rate changes is commonly tested. Vocabulary: collision theory, activation energy, effective collision, orientation, frequency factor, Arrhenius equation, kinetic energy distribution.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-4.C (Unit 5, Topic 5.6) Kinetics - 5.6 Reaction Energy Profile
Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers reaction energy profiles for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-4.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.6). Students should understand that a reaction energy profile (potential energy diagram) shows the energy changes during a reaction. The y-axis is potential energy; the x-axis is reaction coordinate. Key features: reactant energy, product energy, activation energy (Ea, the energy barrier from reactants to transition state), and the enthalpy change (delta-H = products - reactants). Exothermic reactions have products lower than reactants (negative delta-H); endothermic reactions have products higher (positive delta-H). The transition state (activated complex) is at the energy maximum. Catalysts lower Ea without changing delta-H. Students must draw, label, and interpret energy profiles. Connect to thermochemistry, catalysis, and reaction mechanisms. AP exam note: interpreting and drawing energy diagrams is a core skill. Vocabulary: activation energy, transition state, activated complex, exothermic, endothermic, potential energy diagram, reaction coordinate, catalyst.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-5.A (Unit 5, Topic 5.7) Kinetics - 5.7 Introduction to Reaction Mechanisms
Kinetics.prompt: This topic introduces reaction mechanisms for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-5.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.7). Students should understand that a reaction mechanism is a series of elementary steps that shows the molecular-level pathway from reactants to products. The sum of all elementary steps must equal the overall balanced equation. Intermediates are species produced in one step and consumed in a later step (they do not appear in the overall equation). The rate-determining step (RDS) is the slowest step and determines the overall rate law. A valid mechanism must be consistent with the experimentally determined rate law and produce the correct overall equation. Students must identify intermediates, catalysts, and the rate-determining step. Connect to rate laws, elementary reactions, and catalysis. AP exam note: evaluating proposed mechanisms against rate law data is heavily tested. Vocabulary: mechanism, elementary step, intermediate, rate-determining step, catalyst, overall equation, molecularity.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-5.B (Unit 5, Topic 5.8) Kinetics - 5.8 Reaction Mechanism and Rate Law
Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between reaction mechanisms and rate laws for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-5.B, Unit 5, Topic 5.8). Students should understand that the rate law for the overall reaction is determined by the rate-determining step (slow step). If the slow step is the first step, the rate law is written directly from its molecularity. If an intermediate appears in the slow step, its concentration must be expressed in terms of reactants using the equilibrium expression from a preceding fast step (pre-equilibrium assumption). Students must derive overall rate laws from proposed mechanisms and verify consistency with experimental data. A mechanism is plausible only if its derived rate law matches the experimentally observed rate law. Connect to elementary reactions, equilibrium, and kinetics. AP exam note: deriving rate laws from multi-step mechanisms is a challenging but important skill. Vocabulary: rate-determining step, pre-equilibrium, intermediate, rate law derivation, slow step, fast equilibrium step.

This topic covers the pre-equilibrium approximation for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-5.C, Unit 5, Topic 5.9). Students should understand that when a fast reversible step precedes the rate-determining step, the fast step reaches equilibrium before the slow step proceeds. The equilibrium expression from the fast step is used to substitute the intermediate concentration in the rate law of the slow step. For example, if step 1 (fast): A + B <-> C with K = [C]/([A][B]), and step 2 (slow): C -> D with rate = k2[C], then the overall rate = k2*K*[A][B]. Students must apply this substitution technique to derive rate laws consistent with experimental observations. Connect to reaction mechanisms, equilibrium constants, and elementary reactions. AP exam note: multi-step mechanism problems requiring pre-equilibrium substitution are tested in free-response. Vocabulary: pre-equilibrium, intermediate, equilibrium expression, rate-determining step, substitution, fast reversible step.

Kinetics.skill: TRA-5.D (Unit 5, Topic 5.10) Kinetics - 5.10 Multistep Reaction Energy Profile
Kinetics.prompt: This topic covers multistep reaction energy profiles for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-5.D, Unit 5, Topic 5.10). Students should understand that multistep reactions have energy profiles with multiple peaks (transition states) and valleys (intermediates). Each peak represents the transition state of an elementary step, and each valley between peaks represents an intermediate. The highest energy peak corresponds to the rate-determining step (largest activation energy). Students must identify the number of steps, intermediates, transition states, the rate-determining step, and the overall energy change from a multistep energy diagram. The activation energy for the overall reaction is from reactants to the highest transition state. Connect to reaction mechanisms, catalysis, and thermochemistry. AP exam note: interpreting multistep energy profiles is a commonly tested skill. Vocabulary: multistep mechanism, intermediate, transition state, rate-determining step, activation energy, energy profile, potential energy diagram.

This topic covers catalysis for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-1.A, Unit 5, Topic 5.11). Students should understand that catalysts increase reaction rates by providing an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy without being consumed overall. Homogeneous catalysts are in the same phase as reactants; heterogeneous catalysts are in a different phase (often solid catalyst with gaseous or liquid reactants). Enzymes are biological catalysts with high specificity. Catalysts appear in the mechanism (consumed in one step, regenerated in another) but not in the overall equation. They do not change the equilibrium position or the enthalpy change; they speed up both forward and reverse reactions equally. Students must identify catalysts in mechanisms and explain their effect on energy profiles. AP exam note: distinguishing catalysts from intermediates in mechanisms is key. Vocabulary: catalyst, activation energy, homogeneous catalyst, heterogeneous catalyst, enzyme, alternative pathway, reaction intermediate.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.A (Unit 6, Topic 6.1) Thermochemistry - 6.1 Endothermic and Exothermic Processes
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers endothermic and exothermic processes for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.A, Unit 6, Topic 6.1). Students should understand that exothermic processes release heat to the surroundings (delta-H < 0, system loses energy), while endothermic processes absorb heat from the surroundings (delta-H > 0, system gains energy). Energy is conserved: energy lost by the system is gained by surroundings, and vice versa. Examples of exothermic: combustion, neutralization, formation of bonds. Examples of endothermic: photosynthesis, melting, dissolution of ammonium nitrate. Students must identify processes as exothermic or endothermic from enthalpy data or temperature observations. Connect to energy diagrams, bond energies, and Hess's law. AP exam note: correctly assigning signs to delta-H and interpreting energy flow is foundational. Vocabulary: exothermic, endothermic, enthalpy change, system, surroundings, conservation of energy, delta-H, heat.

This topic covers energy diagrams for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.B, Unit 6, Topic 6.2). Students should understand that enthalpy diagrams show the relative enthalpies of reactants and products. For exothermic reactions, products are lower than reactants on the diagram (energy is released). For endothermic reactions, products are higher (energy is absorbed). The difference between reactant and product enthalpy levels equals delta-H. These diagrams differ from activation energy diagrams: enthalpy diagrams do not show transition states or activation energy. Students must draw and interpret enthalpy diagrams, label delta-H, and determine whether a reaction is exothermic or endothermic from the diagram. Connect to Hess's law, bond energies, and thermochemical equations. AP exam note: enthalpy diagram interpretation and construction appear frequently. Vocabulary: enthalpy diagram, enthalpy level, delta-H, exothermic, endothermic, energy released, energy absorbed.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.C (Unit 6, Topic 6.3) Thermochemistry - 6.3 Heat Transfer and Thermal Equilibrium
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers heat transfer and thermal equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.C, Unit 6, Topic 6.3). Students should understand that heat transfers spontaneously from hot objects to cold objects until thermal equilibrium is reached (both at the same temperature). Heat (q) is energy transferred due to temperature difference, while temperature measures average kinetic energy of particles. The law of conservation of energy requires that heat lost by one object equals heat gained by another (q_lost = -q_gained). Students must apply this principle to calorimetry problems and calculate final equilibrium temperatures. Connect to specific heat, calorimetry, and the first law of thermodynamics. AP exam note: heat transfer calculations using conservation of energy are commonly tested. Vocabulary: heat, temperature, thermal equilibrium, heat transfer, exothermic, endothermic, conservation of energy, isolated system.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.D (Unit 6, Topic 6.4) Thermochemistry - 6.4 Heat Capacity and Calorimetry
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers heat capacity and calorimetry for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.D, Unit 6, Topic 6.4). Students should understand that specific heat capacity (c) is the energy required to raise 1 gram of a substance by 1 degree Celsius. The equation q = mc(delta-T) calculates heat absorbed or released. In coffee-cup calorimetry (constant pressure), q_rxn = -q_solution = -mc(delta-T). The sign convention: positive q means heat absorbed, negative q means heat released. Bomb calorimetry measures heat at constant volume. Students must perform calorimetry calculations, determine enthalpy changes per mole, and interpret calorimetry data. Connect to enthalpy of reaction, thermochemical equations, and Hess's law. AP exam note: calorimetry calculations are classic free-response problems. Vocabulary: specific heat capacity, calorimetry, coffee-cup calorimeter, bomb calorimeter, q = mc(delta-T), enthalpy, molar enthalpy.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.E (Unit 6, Topic 6.5) Thermochemistry - 6.5 Energy of Phase Changes
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers energy of phase changes for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.E, Unit 6, Topic 6.5). Students should understand that phase changes occur at constant temperature while energy is absorbed or released. Enthalpy of fusion (delta-H_fus) is the energy for melting (solid to liquid); enthalpy of vaporization (delta-H_vap) is for boiling (liquid to gas). Reverse processes have opposite signs. On a heating curve, plateaus represent phase changes (temperature constant, energy changing phase). Delta-H_vap is larger than delta-H_fus because more intermolecular forces are overcome during vaporization. Students must calculate energy for phase changes using q = n times delta-H and interpret heating curves. Connect to intermolecular forces, calorimetry, and thermochemistry. AP exam note: heating curve analysis and phase change calculations are commonly tested. Vocabulary: enthalpy of fusion, enthalpy of vaporization, heating curve, phase change, plateau, sublimation, condensation.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.F (Unit 6, Topic 6.6) Thermochemistry - 6.6 Introduction to Enthalpy of Reaction
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers enthalpy of reaction for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.F, Unit 6, Topic 6.6). Students should understand that the enthalpy of reaction (delta-H_rxn) is the heat change at constant pressure for a chemical reaction. Thermochemical equations include the delta-H value and specify states of matter. Key principles: delta-H is extensive (proportional to moles), reversing a reaction changes the sign of delta-H, and delta-H depends on states of matter. Standard enthalpy of reaction (delta-H degrees) is measured at standard conditions (25 degrees C, 1 atm, 1 M). Students must interpret thermochemical equations, scale delta-H values for different amounts, and reverse reactions appropriately. Connect to Hess's law, bond enthalpies, and enthalpy of formation. AP exam note: manipulating thermochemical equations is a key skill for Hess's law problems. Vocabulary: enthalpy of reaction, thermochemical equation, standard conditions, extensive property, state function, delta-H.

This topic covers bond enthalpies for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.G, Unit 6, Topic 6.7). Students should understand that bond enthalpy (bond energy) is the energy required to break one mole of a specific bond in the gas phase. Breaking bonds requires energy (endothermic); forming bonds releases energy (exothermic). The enthalpy of reaction can be estimated as: delta-H_rxn = sum of bond enthalpies broken minus sum of bond enthalpies formed. This is an approximation because average bond enthalpies are used, and values vary with molecular environment. If energy to break bonds exceeds energy from forming bonds, the reaction is endothermic. Students must calculate delta-H from bond enthalpy tables and predict whether reactions are exothermic or endothermic. Connect to Lewis structures, molecular stability, and thermochemistry. AP exam note: bond enthalpy calculations appear regularly. Vocabulary: bond enthalpy, bond energy, bond dissociation energy, endothermic, exothermic, average bond enthalpy.

Thermochemistry.skill: ENE-2.H (Unit 6, Topic 6.8) Thermochemistry - 6.8 Enthalpy of Formation
Thermochemistry.prompt: This topic covers enthalpy of formation for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.H, Unit 6, Topic 6.8). Students should understand that the standard enthalpy of formation (delta-H_f degrees) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound forms from its elements in their standard states. By definition, delta-H_f degrees for elements in standard states is zero. The enthalpy of reaction can be calculated using: delta-H_rxn = sum of delta-H_f(products) minus sum of delta-H_f(reactants), with each multiplied by its stoichiometric coefficient. This is a powerful method because extensive tabulated data is available. Students must use formation enthalpies to calculate reaction enthalpies and identify standard states of elements. Connect to Hess's law, thermochemical calculations, and bond enthalpies. AP exam note: formation enthalpy calculations are a core quantitative skill. Vocabulary: standard enthalpy of formation, standard state, delta-H_f, Hess's law, reference point, stoichiometric coefficient.

This topic covers Hess’s law for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-2.I, Unit 6, Topic 6.9). Students should understand that Hess’s law states that the total enthalpy change for a reaction is independent of the pathway and equals the sum of enthalpy changes for individual steps. This follows from enthalpy being a state function. To apply Hess’s law: manipulate given thermochemical equations (reverse, multiply) so they sum to the target equation, then sum the corresponding delta-H values. When reversing an equation, change the sign of delta-H. When multiplying by a factor, multiply delta-H by the same factor. Students must combine multiple thermochemical equations to determine unknown delta-H values. Connect to enthalpy of formation, bond enthalpies, and energy diagrams. AP exam note: Hess’s law problems are classic free-response questions requiring careful equation manipulation. Vocabulary: Hess’s law, state function, thermochemical equation, enthalpy path, additive, manipulation.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-6.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.1) Equilibrium - 7.1 Introduction to Equilibrium
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic introduces equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-6.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.1). Students should understand that chemical equilibrium occurs in reversible reactions when the forward and reverse reaction rates are equal, resulting in constant concentrations of reactants and products. Equilibrium is dynamic: reactions continue in both directions at equal rates. Equilibrium can be reached from either direction and does not mean equal concentrations. Students must recognize the characteristics of equilibrium systems: constant macroscopic properties, ongoing molecular-level changes, and the ability to be approached from either direction. Connect to Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constants, and reaction rates. AP exam note: understanding that equilibrium is dynamic, not static, is essential. Vocabulary: reversible reaction, dynamic equilibrium, forward reaction, reverse reaction, equilibrium state, constant concentrations, closed system.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-6.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.2) Equilibrium - 7.2 Direction of Reversible Reactions
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers the direction of reversible reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-6.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.2). Students should understand that reversible reactions proceed in both directions simultaneously. Initially, only the forward reaction occurs (only reactants present), but as products accumulate, the reverse reaction rate increases until equilibrium is established. The relative rates determine the direction of net change. If forward rate exceeds reverse rate, the reaction shifts toward products. Students must analyze concentration-time graphs to determine when equilibrium is reached and which direction a system is shifting. Connect to reaction rates, equilibrium constants, and Q vs. K analysis. AP exam note: interpreting concentration vs. time graphs at equilibrium is a tested skill. Vocabulary: reversible reaction, forward rate, reverse rate, net reaction, equilibrium position, concentration-time graph.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.3) Equilibrium - 7.3 Reaction Quotient and Equilibrium Constant
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers the reaction quotient and equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.3). Students should understand that the equilibrium constant expression K uses the law of mass action: for aA + bB <-> cC + dD, Kc = [C]^c[D]^d / [A]^a[B]^b. Only aqueous and gaseous species appear; pure solids and liquids are excluded. Kp uses partial pressures instead of concentrations, related by Kp = Kc(RT)^(delta-n). The reaction quotient Q has the same form but uses current (non-equilibrium) concentrations. Comparing Q to K predicts direction: Q < K shifts right (toward products), Q > K shifts left (toward reactants), Q = K at equilibrium. Students must write K expressions and use Q vs. K to predict shifts. AP exam note: Q vs. K analysis is heavily tested. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant, reaction quotient, law of mass action, Kc, Kp, Q, shift, heterogeneous equilibrium.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.4) Equilibrium - 7.4 Calculating the Equilibrium Constant
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers calculating the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.4). Students should understand how to calculate K from equilibrium concentration data using ICE tables (Initial, Change, Equilibrium). Given initial concentrations and one equilibrium value, students can determine all equilibrium concentrations and calculate K. The change row uses stoichiometric ratios with a common variable x. Students must set up ICE tables correctly, solve for x (sometimes requiring the quadratic formula or simplifying assumptions), and calculate K. Approximations are valid when K is very small (x is much less than initial concentration). Connect to Q vs. K analysis, Le Chatelier's principle, and equilibrium calculations. AP exam note: ICE table calculations are classic free-response problems. Vocabulary: ICE table, initial concentration, change, equilibrium concentration, stoichiometric ratio, approximation, quadratic formula.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.C (Unit 7, Topic 7.5) Equilibrium - 7.5 Magnitude of the Equilibrium Constant
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers the magnitude of the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.C, Unit 7, Topic 7.5). Students should understand that the value of K indicates the relative amounts of products and reactants at equilibrium. A large K (much greater than 1) means products are strongly favored; the reaction goes nearly to completion. A small K (much less than 1) means reactants are strongly favored; little product forms. K approximately equal to 1 means significant amounts of both. Students must interpret K values qualitatively and relate them to equilibrium position. The value of K depends only on temperature, not on concentrations, pressures, or catalysts. Connect to thermodynamic favorability, Le Chatelier's principle, and Gibbs free energy. AP exam note: interpreting the magnitude of K and its implications is commonly tested. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant, product-favored, reactant-favored, equilibrium position, extent of reaction, temperature dependence.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.D (Unit 7, Topic 7.6) Equilibrium - 7.6 Properties of the Equilibrium Constant
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers properties of the equilibrium constant for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.D, Unit 7, Topic 7.6). Students should understand how manipulating reactions affects K: reversing a reaction inverts K (K_reverse = 1/K_forward); multiplying a reaction by a factor n raises K to the nth power (K_new = K^n); adding reactions multiplies their K values (K_overall = K1 x K2). K is dimensionless when using activities, but in practice, units may appear depending on the expression. Students must calculate K for modified reactions using these mathematical relationships. Kp and Kc are related by Kp = Kc(RT)^(delta-n) where delta-n is the change in moles of gas. Connect to Hess's law for enthalpy, coupled equilibria, and solubility product. AP exam note: manipulating K values when combining reactions is a tested skill. Vocabulary: equilibrium constant manipulation, inverse, power, product of K values, Kp, Kc, delta-n.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.E (Unit 7, Topic 7.7) Equilibrium - 7.7 Calculating Equilibrium Concentrations
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers calculating equilibrium concentrations for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.E, Unit 7, Topic 7.7). Students should understand how to use ICE tables with known K values to solve for unknown equilibrium concentrations. The process: set up the ICE table with initial concentrations, express changes using stoichiometry and variable x, write the equilibrium expression, substitute, and solve. For small K values, the assumption x is much less than the initial concentration simplifies the algebra (check that x is less than 5 percent of the initial value). For larger K or when the assumption fails, the quadratic formula is needed. Students must also solve for equilibrium concentrations when the system starts at non-equilibrium conditions. Connect to Q vs. K, Le Chatelier's principle, and acid-base equilibrium. AP exam note: ICE table problems are among the most common quantitative questions. Vocabulary: ICE table, equilibrium concentration, small-x approximation, five percent rule, quadratic formula, stoichiometric change.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-7.F (Unit 7, Topic 7.8) Equilibrium - 7.8 Representations of Equilibrium
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers representations of equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-7.F, Unit 7, Topic 7.8). Students should understand how to represent equilibrium at the particulate level using diagrams showing relative numbers of reactant and product molecules. At equilibrium, the ratio of products to reactants is consistent with the K value. Particulate diagrams must conserve atoms and reflect the stoichiometric ratios. Students must interpret particulate diagrams to calculate Q or K, determine if a system is at equilibrium, and predict the direction of shift. Concentration-time graphs showing all species reaching constant values indicate equilibrium. Connect to Q vs. K analysis, Le Chatelier's principle, and dynamic equilibrium. AP exam note: particulate diagram analysis of equilibrium systems is a distinctive skill. Vocabulary: particulate diagram, equilibrium representation, Q vs. K, atom conservation, concentration ratio.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-8.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.9) Equilibrium - 7.9 Introduction to Le Châtelier’s Principle
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic introduces Le Châtelier’s Principle for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-8.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.9). Students should understand that when a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to partially counteract the disturbance and re-establish equilibrium. Adding reactant or removing product shifts the reaction toward products (right); adding product or removing reactant shifts toward reactants (left). Increasing temperature shifts the equilibrium in the endothermic direction; decreasing temperature shifts it in the exothermic direction. Changing pressure (by changing volume) affects gaseous equilibria: increasing pressure shifts toward the side with fewer gas moles. Catalysts do not shift equilibrium; they speed both directions equally. Students must predict and explain the direction of shift for various disturbances. AP exam note: Le Châtelier’s principle is one of the most heavily tested topics. Vocabulary: Le Châtelier’s principle, stress, shift, equilibrium position, concentration change, temperature change, pressure change.

Equilibrium.skill: TRA-8.B (Unit 7, Topic 7.10) Equilibrium - 7.10 Reaction Quotient and Le Châtelier’s Principle
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between Q and Le Châtelier’s Principle for AP Chemistry students (CED: TRA-8.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.10). Students should understand that Le Châtelier’s principle can be explained quantitatively using Q vs. K analysis. When a stress is applied, Q changes relative to K: adding reactant decreases Q (Q < K, shifts right); adding product increases Q (Q > K, shifts left); removing reactant increases Q; removing product decreases Q. Temperature changes actually change K itself (K increases for endothermic reactions when heated, decreases for exothermic). Volume changes affect Q for gaseous reactions with unequal moles of gas. Students must use Q vs. K to justify predicted shifts and calculate new equilibrium positions. Connect to equilibrium calculations and thermodynamics. AP exam note: connecting Q vs. K to Le Châtelier’s qualitative predictions strengthens analysis. Vocabulary: reaction quotient Q, equilibrium constant K, stress, shift direction, new equilibrium, concentration perturbation.

Equilibrium.skill: SPQ-5.A (Unit 7, Topic 7.11) Equilibrium - 7.11 Introduction to Solubility Equilibria
Equilibrium.prompt: This topic introduces solubility equilibria for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-5.A, Unit 7, Topic 7.11). Students should understand that the dissolution of a slightly soluble ionic compound reaches an equilibrium described by the solubility product constant Ksp. For compound AB2 dissolving as AB2(s) <-> A2+(aq) + 2B-(aq), Ksp = [A2+][B-]^2. The solid does not appear in the expression. Ksp values allow calculation of molar solubility (mol/L that dissolve) and comparison of relative solubilities of compounds with the same ion ratio. Precipitation occurs when Q > Ksp (ion product exceeds solubility product). Students must write Ksp expressions, calculate molar solubility from Ksp, and predict whether precipitation occurs. Connect to equilibrium principles, Q vs. K, and ionic compounds. AP exam note: Ksp calculations and precipitation predictions are common. Vocabulary: solubility product, Ksp, molar solubility, ion product, precipitation, slightly soluble, saturated solution.

This topic covers the common-ion effect for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-5.B, Unit 7, Topic 7.12). Students should understand that adding a common ion (an ion already present in the equilibrium) to a solution of a slightly soluble salt decreases its solubility. This is an application of Le Chatelier's principle: the added common ion increases Q above Ksp, causing the equilibrium to shift left (toward precipitation). For example, adding NaCl to a saturated AgCl solution decreases AgCl solubility because [Cl-] increases. Students must calculate molar solubility in the presence of a common ion using modified ICE tables with Ksp. Connect to Le Chatelier's principle, precipitation reactions, and buffer chemistry. AP exam note: common-ion effect calculations and qualitative predictions are tested. Vocabulary: common-ion effect, molar solubility, Le Chatelier's principle, Ksp, ion product, precipitation, solubility decrease.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.A (Unit 8, Topic 8.1) Acids and Bases - 8.1 Introduction to Acids and Bases
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic introduces acids and bases for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.1). Students should understand three acid-base definitions: Arrhenius (acids produce H+, bases produce OH- in water), Bronsted-Lowry (acids donate protons, bases accept protons), and Lewis (acids accept electron pairs, bases donate electron pairs). Water autoionizes: 2H2O <-> H3O+ + OH-, with Kw = [H3O+][OH-] = 1.0 x 10^-14 at 25 degrees C. In any aqueous solution, the product of [H3O+] and [OH-] equals Kw. Strong acids and bases dissociate completely; weak acids and bases partially dissociate. Students must identify acids, bases, and conjugate pairs using each definition. Connect to pH, equilibrium, buffers, and titration. AP exam note: applying all three definitions and understanding Kw is essential. Vocabulary: Arrhenius, Bronsted-Lowry, Lewis, conjugate pair, autoionization, Kw, amphoteric, hydronium, hydroxide.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.B (Unit 8, Topic 8.2) Acids and Bases - 8.2 pH and pOH of Strong Acids and Bases
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers pH and pOH of strong acids and bases for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.2). Students should understand that pH = -log[H3O+], pOH = -log[OH-], and pH + pOH = 14 at 25 degrees C. Strong acids (HCl, HBr, HI, HNO3, H2SO4, HClO4) completely dissociate, so [H3O+] equals the acid concentration. Strong bases (Group 1 and heavy Group 2 hydroxides) completely dissociate, so [OH-] equals the base concentration (or twice for Ba(OH)2, Ca(OH)2). pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is basic. Students must calculate pH and pOH from strong acid/base concentrations and vice versa, and work with the logarithmic pH scale. Connect to weak acid/base equilibria, buffers, and titration. AP exam note: pH calculations for strong acids and bases are foundational. Vocabulary: pH, pOH, strong acid, strong base, complete dissociation, logarithmic scale, hydronium concentration, hydroxide concentration.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.C (Unit 8, Topic 8.3) Acids and Bases - 8.3 Weak Acid and Base Equilibria
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers weak acid and base equilibria for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.3).
