Students should understand that weak acids partially dissociate in water with equilibrium constant Ka, and weak bases partially react with water with equilibrium constant Kb. For HA <-> H+ + A-, Ka = [H+][A-]/[HA]. Larger Ka means stronger weak acid. For conjugate acid-base pairs, Ka x Kb = Kw. ICE tables are used to calculate [H+], pH, and percent dissociation from Ka and initial concentration. The small-x approximation is valid when Ka is very small relative to initial concentration. Percent ionization increases as concentration decreases. Students must perform equilibrium calculations for weak acids and bases. Connect to pH, buffers, Henderson-Hasselbalch, and titration. AP exam note: ICE table calculations for weak acids and bases are heavily tested. Vocabulary: weak acid, weak base, Ka, Kb, percent ionization, conjugate pair, ICE table, partial dissociation, equilibrium.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.D (Unit 8, Topic 8.4) Acids and Bases - 8.4 Acid-Base Reactions and Buffers
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers acid-base reactions and buffers for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.D, Unit 8, Topic 8.4). Students should understand that when a strong acid reacts with a strong base, the net ionic equation is H+ + OH- -> H2O, and the equivalence point has pH 7. When a weak acid reacts with a strong base (or vice versa), the conjugate base (or acid) formed affects the pH at equivalence. Buffers resist pH changes and consist of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or weak base and conjugate acid). Buffers work by neutralizing small additions of acid or base through equilibrium shifts. Students must predict the pH at equivalence for different acid-base combinations and explain buffer action. Connect to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, titration curves, and buffer capacity. AP exam note: understanding buffer composition and action is essential. Vocabulary: buffer, conjugate pair, buffer action, neutralization, equivalence point, strong-weak combination, salt hydrolysis.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.E (Unit 8, Topic 8.5) Acids and Bases - 8.5 Acid-Base Titrations
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers acid-base titrations for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.E, Unit 8, Topic 8.5). Students should understand the shapes of titration curves for strong acid-strong base (sharp equivalence at pH 7), weak acid-strong base (equivalence pH > 7 due to conjugate base hydrolysis), and strong acid-weak base (equivalence pH < 7). Key points on a titration curve: initial pH, buffer region, half-equivalence point (pH = pKa for weak acid titrations), equivalence point, and post-equivalence region. At the half-equivalence point, [HA] = [A-] and pH = pKa. Students must interpret titration curves, identify equivalence points, select appropriate indicators, and calculate pH at various points. Connect to buffers, Henderson-Hasselbalch, and Ka/Kb calculations. AP exam note: titration curve analysis is one of the most tested topics. Vocabulary: titration curve, equivalence point, half-equivalence point, buffer region, indicator, inflection point, endpoint.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-9.F (Unit 8, Topic 8.6) Acids and Bases - 8.6 Molecular Structure of Acids and Bases
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers molecular structure of acids and bases for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-9.F, Unit 8, Topic 8.6). Students should understand how molecular structure affects acid and base strength. For binary acids (HX): acid strength increases with decreasing bond strength (going down a group, e.g., HF < HCl < HBr < HI) because weaker bonds release H+ more easily. For oxyacids: acid strength increases with more electronegative atoms or more oxygen atoms bonded to the central atom, as these stabilize the conjugate base by delocalizing the negative charge. Stronger acids have more stable conjugate bases. For bases, stronger bases have weaker conjugate acids. Students must predict and explain relative acid strengths from molecular structure. Connect to electronegativity, bond polarity, and resonance stabilization. AP exam note: ranking acid strength from structure is a key analytical skill. Vocabulary: binary acid, oxyacid, bond strength, electronegativity, resonance stabilization, conjugate base stability, acid strength.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-10.A (Unit 8, Topic 8.7) Acids and Bases - 8.7 pH and pKa
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers pH and pKa for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-10.A, Unit 8, Topic 8.7). Students should understand that pKa = -log(Ka) and a lower pKa indicates a stronger acid. The relationship between pH and pKa determines the dominant form of a weak acid in solution: when pH < pKa, the protonated form (HA) dominates; when pH > pKa, the deprotonated form (A-) dominates; when pH = pKa, both forms are present in equal concentrations. This is the basis of the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. Students must use pKa values to compare acid strengths and predict the predominant species at a given pH. Connect to buffers, titration curves, and biological systems where pH controls molecular form. AP exam note: using pKa to determine dominant species is a commonly tested skill. Vocabulary: pKa, acid strength, dominant species, protonated form, deprotonated form, Henderson-Hasselbalch, conjugate pair.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-10.B (Unit 8, Topic 8.8) Acids and Bases - 8.8 Properties of Buffers
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers properties of buffers for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-10.B, Unit 8, Topic 8.8). Students should understand that a buffer is a solution containing a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid) in significant concentrations. Buffers maintain relatively constant pH by neutralizing added acid (the conjugate base reacts with H+) or added base (the weak acid donates H+ to OH-). Effective buffers have a pH within one unit of the pKa and roughly equal concentrations of the weak acid and conjugate base. The buffer range is typically pKa plus or minus 1. Students must identify buffer solutions, explain how they resist pH changes, and predict the effect of adding acid or base. Connect to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, titration, and biological buffer systems. AP exam note: identifying buffers and explaining their mechanism is essential. Vocabulary: buffer, conjugate pair, buffer range, buffer action, weak acid, conjugate base, pH stability.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-10.C (Unit 8, Topic 8.9) Acids and Bases - 8.9 Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-10.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.9). Students should understand that the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation pH = pKa + log([A-]/[HA]) allows direct calculation of buffer pH from the concentrations of the weak acid and conjugate base. When [A-] = [HA], pH = pKa. When the ratio [A-]/[HA] increases, pH increases; when it decreases, pH decreases. This equation can also be used to determine the ratio needed for a target pH. Students must calculate buffer pH, determine required ratios for desired pH, and apply the equation at the half-equivalence point of a titration. The equation assumes the small-x approximation is valid. Connect to buffer properties, titration curves, and biological pH control. AP exam note: Henderson-Hasselbalch calculations appear frequently. Vocabulary: Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, pKa, conjugate acid-base ratio, buffer pH, half-equivalence point, logarithmic relationship.

Acids and Bases.skill: SAP-10.D (Unit 8, Topic 8.10) Acids and Bases - 8.10 Buffer Capacity
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers buffer capacity for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-10.D, Unit 8, Topic 8.10). Students should understand that buffer capacity is the amount of strong acid or base a buffer can neutralize before a significant pH change occurs. Buffer capacity increases with higher concentrations of the weak acid and conjugate base components. A buffer is most effective when [HA] is approximately equal to [A-] (pH approximately equal to pKa). When one component is consumed, the buffer is exhausted and pH changes rapidly. Students must calculate how much acid or base a buffer can absorb, predict when a buffer will fail, and compare the capacities of different buffer solutions. Connect to Henderson-Hasselbalch equation, titration equivalence, and laboratory buffer preparation. AP exam note: questions may ask students to compare buffer capacities or predict buffer failure. Vocabulary: buffer capacity, buffer exhaustion, moles of acid/base neutralized, concentration effect, effective buffer range.

Acids and Bases.skill: SPQ-5.C (Unit 8, Topic 8.11) Acids and Bases - 8.11 pH and Solubility
Acids and Bases.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between pH and solubility for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-5.C, Unit 8, Topic 8.11). Students should understand that pH affects the solubility of salts containing basic anions. For example, metal hydroxides, carbonates, sulfides, and phosphates are more soluble in acidic solutions because H+ reacts with the basic anion, removing it from the Ksp equilibrium and shifting it toward dissolution. Adding acid to a solution of Ca(OH)2 neutralizes OH-, increasing solubility. Salts of strong acid anions (Cl-, NO3-) are generally unaffected by pH because these anions do not react with H+. Students must predict how pH changes affect solubility and explain using Le Chatelier's principle and Ksp. Connect to solubility equilibria, common-ion effect, and acid-base chemistry. AP exam note: predicting pH effects on solubility is tested. Vocabulary: solubility, Ksp, basic anion, acidic solution, Le Chatelier's principle, dissolution, pH dependence.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-4.A (Unit 9, Topic 9.1) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.1 Introduction to Entropy
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic introduces entropy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.1). Students should understand that entropy (S) is a measure of the number of possible microstates (molecular arrangements) of a system, often described as a measure of disorder or energy dispersal. Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, the number of particles increases (reactions producing more moles of gas), or complex molecules form from simpler ones. The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of the universe always increases for spontaneous processes. Students must predict the sign of delta-S for given processes and compare entropy of different states. Connect to Gibbs free energy, spontaneity, and thermodynamic favorability. AP exam note: predicting entropy changes qualitatively is commonly tested. Vocabulary: entropy, microstates, disorder, energy dispersal, second law, spontaneous, delta-S, positional entropy.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-4.B (Unit 9, Topic 9.2) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.2 Absolute Entropy and Entropy Change
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers absolute entropy and entropy change for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.2). Students should understand that the third law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a perfect crystal at absolute zero (0 K) is zero. This allows measurement of absolute (standard) molar entropy values S-degrees for all substances. Entropy change for a reaction is calculated as: delta-S_rxn = sum of S-degrees(products) minus sum of S-degrees(reactants), weighted by stoichiometric coefficients. Unlike enthalpy of formation, absolute entropy values are never zero for elements. Larger, more complex molecules have higher absolute entropies. Students must calculate delta-S from standard entropy tables and predict signs. Connect to Gibbs free energy, spontaneity, and entropy concepts. AP exam note: calculating delta-S from standard entropy values is a tested quantitative skill. Vocabulary: absolute entropy, third law, standard molar entropy, delta-S, perfect crystal, stoichiometric calculation.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-4.C (Unit 9, Topic 9.3) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.3 Gibbs Free Energy and Thermodynamic Favorability
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers Gibbs free energy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.3). Students should understand that Gibbs free energy change (delta-G = delta-H - T*delta-S) determines thermodynamic favorability (spontaneity). When delta-G < 0, the process is thermodynamically favorable (spontaneous). When delta-G > 0, it is unfavorable. When delta-G = 0, the system is at equilibrium. There are four enthalpy-entropy combinations: both favorable (always spontaneous), both unfavorable (never spontaneous), and two temperature-dependent cases. Delta-G-degrees can be calculated from delta-H-degrees and delta-S-degrees, or from standard free energies of formation. Students must calculate delta-G, determine favorability, and identify the temperature at which favorability changes (T = delta-H/delta-S). AP exam note: Gibbs free energy calculations and favorability predictions are heavily tested. Vocabulary: Gibbs free energy, thermodynamic favorability, spontaneous, nonspontaneous, delta-G, delta-H, delta-S, temperature dependence.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-4.D (Unit 9, Topic 9.4) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.4 Thermodynamic and Kinetic Control
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers thermodynamic and kinetic control for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-4.D, Unit 9, Topic 9.4). Students should understand the distinction between thermodynamic favorability and kinetic feasibility. A reaction can be thermodynamically favorable (delta-G < 0) but kinetically slow due to a high activation energy barrier. Diamond converting to graphite is thermodynamically favorable but kinetically controlled (extremely slow). Conversely, a thermodynamically unfavorable reaction will not proceed regardless of kinetics. Catalysts affect kinetics but not thermodynamics. Students must explain why some favorable reactions do not occur at observable rates and distinguish between thermodynamic and kinetic arguments. Connect to activation energy, catalysis, and Gibbs free energy. AP exam note: explaining why thermodynamically favorable reactions may not be observed is important. Vocabulary: thermodynamic control, kinetic control, activation energy, kinetic stability, thermodynamic stability, catalyst, spontaneity, rate.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-5.A (Unit 9, Topic 9.5) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.5 Free Energy and Equilibrium
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers the relationship between free energy and equilibrium for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.5). Students should understand that delta-G-degrees and K are related by delta-G-degrees = -RT*ln(K). When delta-G-degrees < 0, K > 1 (products favored at equilibrium). When delta-G-degrees > 0, K < 1 (reactants favored). When delta-G-degrees = 0, K = 1. At non-standard conditions, delta-G = delta-G-degrees + RT*ln(Q). At equilibrium, delta-G = 0 (not delta-G-degrees). Students must calculate K from delta-G-degrees and vice versa, and explain the relationship between free energy and equilibrium position. Connect to equilibrium constants, Le Chatelier's principle, and thermodynamics. AP exam note: the delta-G and K relationship is a key conceptual and quantitative topic. Vocabulary: Gibbs free energy, equilibrium constant, delta-G-degrees, reaction quotient, standard conditions, non-standard conditions.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-5.B (Unit 9, Topic 9.6) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.6 Free Energy of Dissolution
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers free energy of dissolution for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.6). Students should understand that the dissolution of a salt involves both enthalpy and entropy changes. Lattice energy (energy to separate ions, endothermic) and hydration energy (energy released when ions interact with water, exothermic) determine delta-H of dissolution. Entropy generally increases upon dissolution (more dispersed ions and solvent molecules). Dissolution is thermodynamically favorable when delta-G < 0, which depends on the balance of delta-H and T*delta-S. Some endothermic dissolutions are spontaneous because the entropy increase overcomes the unfavorable enthalpy. Students must explain solubility in terms of free energy and the balance of enthalpic and entropic factors. Connect to Ksp, intermolecular forces, and lattice energy. AP exam note: explaining why some salts dissolve despite positive delta-H is tested. Vocabulary: dissolution, lattice energy, hydration energy, entropy of dissolution, free energy, Ksp, spontaneous dissolution.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-5.C (Unit 9, Topic 9.7) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.7 Coupled Reactions
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers coupled reactions for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-5.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.7). Students should understand that a thermodynamically unfavorable reaction (positive delta-G) can be driven forward by coupling it with a strongly favorable reaction (very negative delta-G) so that the overall delta-G is negative. The most important biological example is ATP hydrolysis (delta-G approximately -30.5 kJ/mol) driving otherwise unfavorable biochemical reactions. In chemistry, coupling reactions is applied in extractive metallurgy and industrial synthesis. The overall delta-G equals the sum of individual delta-G values (analogous to Hess's law). Students must calculate overall delta-G for coupled reactions and determine whether the coupled process is favorable. Connect to Hess's law, Gibbs free energy, and biological energetics. AP exam note: coupled reaction problems test understanding of additivity of delta-G. Vocabulary: coupled reactions, thermodynamically unfavorable, driving force, ATP hydrolysis, overall delta-G, Hess's law, free energy additivity.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-6.A (Unit 9, Topic 9.8) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.8 Galvanic (Voltaic) and Electrolytic Cells
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers galvanic and electrolytic cells for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.A, Unit 9, Topic 9.8). Students should understand that galvanic (voltaic) cells convert chemical energy to electrical energy spontaneously (delta-G < 0, positive E-cell). The anode is the site of oxidation (negative terminal), the cathode is the site of reduction (positive terminal), and a salt bridge maintains electrical neutrality. Electrolytic cells use external electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions (delta-G > 0, negative E-cell). In electrolytic cells, the anode is positive and the cathode is negative (externally forced). Students must diagram both cell types, label components, identify anode/cathode, write half-reactions, and determine electron flow direction. Connect to redox reactions, cell potential, and Faraday's law. AP exam note: comparing galvanic and electrolytic cells is heavily tested. Vocabulary: galvanic cell, electrolytic cell, anode, cathode, salt bridge, half-reaction, oxidation, reduction, electrode, external circuit.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-6.B (Unit 9, Topic 9.9) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.9 Cell Potential and Free Energy
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers cell potential and free energy for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.B, Unit 9, Topic 9.9). Students should understand that standard cell potential E-degrees-cell = E-degrees-cathode - E-degrees-anode, using standard reduction potentials from a reference table. A positive E-degrees-cell indicates a spontaneous reaction. The relationship between cell potential and free energy is delta-G-degrees = -nFE-degrees-cell, where n is moles of electrons transferred and F is Faraday's constant (96485 C/mol). This connects electrochemistry to thermodynamics. Students must calculate E-degrees-cell from half-reaction potentials, determine spontaneity, and convert between E-degrees, delta-G-degrees, and K. The relationship delta-G-degrees = -RT*ln(K) links all three quantities. AP exam note: calculating and interpreting cell potentials is a core skill. Vocabulary: standard cell potential, standard reduction potential, Faraday's constant, spontaneous, nonspontaneous, half-reaction, delta-G, equilibrium constant.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-6.C (Unit 9, Topic 9.10) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.10 Cell Potential Under Nonstandard Conditions
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers cell potential under nonstandard conditions for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.C, Unit 9, Topic 9.10). Students should understand the Nernst equation: E = E-degrees - (RT/nF)*ln(Q), which at 25 degrees C simplifies to E = E-degrees - (0.0592/n)*log(Q). When Q < K, E is positive and the reaction proceeds forward. When Q > K, E is negative. At equilibrium, Q = K and E = 0. Changes in concentration affect cell potential: increasing reactant concentration or decreasing product concentration increases E. As a galvanic cell operates, concentrations change, Q approaches K, and E decreases to zero (cell is dead). Students must calculate E under nonstandard conditions and predict how concentration changes affect voltage. Connect to equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, and battery operation. AP exam note: Nernst equation calculations are tested. Vocabulary: Nernst equation, nonstandard conditions, reaction quotient Q, cell potential, concentration cell, equilibrium.

Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.skill: ENE-6.D (Unit 9, Topic 9.11) Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry - 9.11 Electrolysis and Faraday’s Law
Thermodynamics and Electrochemistry.prompt: This topic covers electrolysis and Faraday’s law for AP Chemistry students (CED: ENE-6.D, Unit 9, Topic 9.11). Students should understand that electrolysis uses electrical energy to drive nonspontaneous reactions, such as electroplating metals, decomposing compounds, and producing elements (chlor-alkali process, aluminum smelting). Faraday’s law relates the amount of substance produced to the charge passed: moles of substance = (current x time) / (n x F), where current is in amperes, time in seconds, n is electrons per formula unit, and F = 96485 C/mol. Students must calculate the mass of substance deposited or produced given current and time, and determine the time or current needed for a desired amount. Connect to redox stoichiometry, cell potential, and industrial applications. AP exam note: Faraday’s law calculations are commonly tested. Vocabulary: electrolysis, Faraday’s law, coulombs, amperes, electroplating, current, charge, moles of electrons, stoichiometry.
